📚 Microbe Library

Browse the full collection of beneficial microbes. Filter by function, crop, or environment, or search by name. Tap any microbe for detailed profiles, dosage, and DIY propagation guides.

📚 Documented for [crop] means we have research or extension citations for this pairing. Most microbes work on many crops — this filter is a starting point, not an exclusion list.

🌾 AI Farm Advisor

Describe your crop problem — symptoms, environment, what you've tried. AMIS will diagnose the likely cause and rank the microbes most relevant to your situation. Works offline with built-in agronomy rules, or online with AI for nuanced cases.

Product information
Type the numbers from your label, e.g. label says 1 × 10⁸ CFU/mL → enter 1 and 8
× 10 CFU per
= 1 × 10⁸ CFU/mL  (liquid product)
For products without clear CFU info — type the dilution ratio printed on the label.
1 : e.g. 500 for 1:500 dilution
% e.g. 0.2 for 0.2% (= 1:500)
= 1 : 500 dilution (2 mL of product per L of water)
Note: In dilution mode, disease-pressure adjustments don\'t apply — the label rate is what the manufacturer tested. For outbreak situations, spray more frequently rather than concentrating beyond the label.
💡 Tip: A dilution ratio tells you how to mix one tank, but not how potent the product is. Two products with the same 1:500 ratio can deliver very different amounts of live microbes if their CFU counts differ. Compare the CFU between products — that\'s what you\'re really paying for.
Programme length (weeks — affects seasonal totals)
Show methods
Disease / pest pressure (adjusts dose: lower for routine, higher for active outbreak)
Methods I have access to (filters out microbes I can't apply)

🩺 First Aid — Sick Tree Triage

Pick your crop and how urgent the situation is. AMIS will give you honest, prioritised actions — including when microbes are NOT the answer. This is field triage, not diagnostic certainty. If your tree is at risk, don't wait for perfect information; act on the most likely cause.

💡 Foliar spray failed? Check these first: 1) Timing: Sprayed midday (9 AM–4 PM) in sun? UV destroys some fungi (Trichoderma, Beauveria, Metarhizium) and sensitive bacteria. Next time: spray before 8 AM or after 4 PM. 2) Protection: Add Sodium Lignosulfonate (1 g/L) to extend viability 3–5×. 3) Sticker: Use sticker oil (Tween 20, 0.5–1 mL/L) so spray sticks to leaves. See Tank-Mix Builder for complete setup.
Step 1 — Which crop?
Step 2 — How urgent?

Microbial Synergy Guide

Combining the right microbes multiplies their effect. The wrong combination wastes product or causes antagonism. Use this guide to build effective consortia.

How to read synergy ratings
Strong — Apply together, effects multiply (2–5× better than solo)
Moderate — Compatible, minor additive benefit. Apply 24–48 h apart is fine
Neutral / Separate — No conflict but apply in different slots (morning vs evening, or separate weeks)
Avoid — Competitive or antagonistic. One suppresses the other

⚗️ Heavy Metal Microbe Matrix

Microbes that tolerate or actively remediate heavy metal contamination. Useful for ex-mining land, soils near industrial areas, or fields with copper/zinc excess from repeated pesticide use.

⚠️ Important: Bioremediation is a long-term process (months to years). Always conduct a soil test before starting a remediation programme. These microbes assist — they do not replace proper soil management.
Export the entire matrix:
🔗 Microbial Chelation Mechanisms

A complete reference for all 6 pathways microbes use to chelate, mobilise, and detoxify metals and minerals in soil. Each pathway is distinct — the most effective programs combine multiple mechanisms simultaneously.

M1 Organic Acid Chelation Most common in soil
Microbes secrete organic acids that donate electrons (H⁺) to metal ions, forming soluble metal-acid complexes that roots can absorb. The acid lowers local pH which further increases metal solubility.

Acid Key Microbes Metals
CitricAspergillus niger, Yarrowia, PenicilliumCa, Fe, P, Zn, Cu, Al
GluconicB. megaterium, Pseudomonas, GluconobacterP, Ca, Fe, Mn, Zn
2-KetogluconicP. fluorescens, BurkholderiaTricalcium phosphate, Al, Fe
OxalicTrichoderma, AspergillusCa, Cu, Pb → immobilises as oxalate
LacticLactobacillus, B. coagulansCa, Mg, Fe, Mn
MalicB. subtilis, RhizobiumFe, Al (acid soils)
AceticGluconacetobacter, EM-1Ca (dissolves CaCO₃)
Succinic / FumaricRhizobium, RhodopseudomonasFe, Ca, Mg
Continues working? Consumed over time — replenish by keeping microbial population active with organic carbon (compost, molasses, humic acid).
M2 Siderophore Chelation Self-recycling ♻️
Microbes secrete small, high-affinity molecules (siderophores) that lock onto Fe³⁺ with extraordinary strength (Ka ~10³²) — then deliver it to the plant cell. The siderophore is recycled by the microbe after metal delivery, making this a continuous, self-sustaining chelation system.

Pyoverdine
Producer: Pseudomonas fluorescens, P. putida · Targets: Fe, Cu, Mn, Zn · Recycled by microbe ✅
Desferrioxamine
Producer: Streptomyces griseus, S. lydicus · Targets: Fe, Zn, Cu · Recycled ✅
Bacillibactin
Producer: Bacillus subtilis, B. amyloliquefaciens · Targets: Fe, Mn · Recycled ✅
Pyochelin
Producer: Pseudomonas aeruginosa · Targets: Fe, Cr, Cd · Secondary siderophore under Fe stress
Continues working? ✅ Yes — siderophores are recovered and reused. Active as long as the microbial population is alive.
M3 EPS Matrix Chelation Persists after death 💀
Exopolysaccharides (EPS) are sticky gel matrices secreted around microbial cells. The carboxyl (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups in EPS carry a net negative charge that electrostatically traps positively charged metal cations. EPS biofilm persists even after the cells die — it keeps binding metals passively.

Azotobacter chroococcum
Thickest EPS of any free-living bacterium · Cu, Pb, Cd, Zn
Rhizobium spp.
EPS required for nodule formation · Fe, Mn, Cu trapping
Bacillus subtilis
Biofilm EPS + levan polymer · Cu²⁺, Pb²⁺, Cr³⁺
Stenotrophomonas
Multi-layer EPS · Cu, Zn, Cd in industrial-affected soils
Continues working? ✅ Yes — EPS matrix persists in soil for months to years after cells die. Best long-term passive chelation system.
M4 Reductive Chelation Changes metal state 🔄
Microbes use electron transfer (reduction) to convert metals from insoluble oxidised forms to soluble reduced forms — or vice versa to immobilise toxic metals. Fundamentally different from acid chelation — no pH change needed.

Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ (iron reduction) — Makes insoluble Fe(OH)₃ into soluble Fe²⁺ for plant uptake
Microbes: Pseudomonas, Bacillus, Rhodopseudomonas palustris
Cr(VI) → Cr(III) (chromate reduction) — Toxic Cr(VI) → less toxic, immobile Cr(III)
Microbes: Arthrobacter globiformis (chromate reductase enzyme)
Mn⁴⁺ → Mn²⁺ (manganese reduction) — Unlocks Mn for enzyme cofactor use
Microbes: Bacillus megaterium, Pseudomonas spp.
As(V) → As(III) (arsenate reduction) — Mobilises As for phytoextraction
Microbes: Pseudomonas fluorescens, Bacillus spp.
Continues working? ✅ Yes — as long as microbe is metabolically active. Enzyme-driven, not consumed.
M5 Phytochelatin Induction Plant-activated 🌱
Certain microbes trigger the plant's own chelation genes — stimulating the plant to produce phytochelatins (PC) and metallothioneins (MT) internally. These plant proteins bind heavy metals inside cells, preventing toxicity. The microbe is the activator; the plant does the chelation work.

5-ALA production — stimulates Fe/Mg chelation and chlorophyll synthesis
Microbes: Rhodopseudomonas palustris, Rhodobacter sphaeroides
IAA (auxin) signalling — upregulates phytochelatin synthase gene (PCS1)
Microbes: Azospirillum brasilense, Bacillus subtilis, Pseudomonas
Salicylate / jasmonate induction — systemic metal stress response activation
Microbes: Bacillus velezensis, Trichoderma asperellum (ISR pathway)
Ethylene modulation (ACC deaminase) — reduces metal-induced ethylene stress
Microbes: Pseudomonas putida, Variovorax paradoxus, Phyllobacterium
Continues working? ✅ Yes — plant continuously produces chelators while microbe signal persists. Builds up over growing season.
M6 Volatile Chelation Gas-phase action 💨
Microbial volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and gases compete with metals for binding sites or directly chelate metals in the gas phase — effective across soil distances without direct contact.

HCN (hydrogen cyanide) — chelates Fe³⁺ competitively, depriving pathogens of Fe
Microbes: Pseudomonas fluorescens, P. aeruginosa
NH₃ (ammonia) — raises local pH, precipitates Cu, Zn, Ni as hydroxides
Microbes: Bacillus spp. (urea hydrolysis), Azotobacter
H₂S (hydrogen sulphide) — precipitates Cd, Pb, Zn, Cu as insoluble sulphides
Microbes: Sulphate-reducing bacteria (waterlogged / flooded soils)
2,3-Butanediol / Acetoin — VOCs that promote ISR and indirectly reduce metal stress signalling
Microbes: Bacillus subtilis, B. amyloliquefaciens
Continues working? ⚠️ Intermittent — depends on metabolic activity and soil conditions. H₂S only in anaerobic conditions.
Quick Reference
Mechanism Continuous? Speed Mobilises or Immobilises? Best metals
M1 Organic Acid⚠️ Needs carbonFastBothP, Fe, Ca, Cu, Zn
M2 Siderophore✅ RecycledFastMobilisesFe, Cu, Mn, Zn
M3 EPS Matrix✅ PersistsSlowImmobilisesCu, Pb, Cd, Zn, Cr
M4 Reductive✅ EnzymaticMediumBothFe, Cr, Mn, As
M5 Phytochelatin✅ Via plantSlow buildImmobilises in plantCd, Pb, Cu, Hg
M6 Volatile⚠️ IntermittentFastImmobilisesFe, Cu, Cd, Pb, Zn
Best multi-mechanism stack: Pseudomonas fluorescens (M1+M2+M4+M6) + Azotobacter chroococcum (M1+M3) + Rhodopseudomonas palustris (M4+M5) covers 5 of 6 mechanisms simultaneously — the most complete chelation programme possible with commercial products.
🧪 Enhance Detox Effect — Combine With

These soil amendments work synergistically with bioremediation microbes — they bind, immobilise, or chelate metals while the microbes break down contaminants biologically.

🟫 Humic Acid Metal Binding
Forms stable complexes with Cu²⁺, Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺ and Zn²⁺ — reduces plant-available toxic fraction. Also feeds microbial biomass, boosting remediation microbe populations by 2–3×. Apply at 5–10 kg/acre with microbe drench.
🟡 Fulvic Acid Chelating Agent
Low molecular weight — penetrates cell walls and chelates metals inside plant tissue, aiding phytoextraction. Pairs well with Pseudomonas putida and Sphingomonas to mobilise metals from soil for plant uptake. Apply at 2–5 kg/acre foliar or drench.
⬛ Biochar Metal Immobilisation
High surface area and negative charge adsorbs Cd, Pb, Cu, and As — physically immobilising them in soil matrix. Raises soil pH (reduces metal solubility) and provides long-term habitat for remediation microbes. Apply at 1–2 tonnes/acre incorporated into top 15 cm. Effect lasts 5–10 years.
🟤 Sodium Lignosulfonate Chelating Support
Lignin-based chelator that complexes Cu, Fe, and Mn — reducing phytotoxicity from copper fungicide buildup. Also acts as a dispersant, improving microbe suspension stability in spray tanks. Apply at 1–2 kg/acre mixed with microbe drench.
🌿 Compost / Organic Matter Microbial Carrier
High organic matter dilutes metal concentration per unit volume and feeds the microbial community. Stabilises soil pH and provides carbon source for heterotrophic remediation microbes. Acts as a carrier matrix for microbe inoculants — mix microbes into finished compost before application. Apply at 2–5 tonnes/acre.
Recommended stack for ex-mining land: Biochar (immobilise) + Humic acid (bind) + Arthrobacter globiformis or Pseudomonas putida (biodegrade) + Compost (carrier). Apply in that order over 3–6 months.
📋 Malaysian Context
Ex-mining land (Tanah bekas lombong) — typically contaminated with As, Cd, Pb, Zn from tin mining. Common in Perak, Selangor, Pahang.
Copper excess — from repeated copper-based fungicide use in durian, cocoa, and rubber. Builds up in topsoil over decades.
Industrial runoff — Cr, Pb near electroplating, battery, and textile industries. Check proximity to industrial zones.
Oil palm & rubber — heavy herbicide and fertiliser history; zinc and copper accumulation common.

🧪 Brew Safety & Disposal Triage

Identify what went wrong with a failed microbial brew and dispose of it safely.

A failed microbial brew is not just smelly waste — it may contain pathogenic contaminants (Clostridia, mycotoxin-producing molds, enteric bacteria) that cannot be safely dumped on crops or into drains. Use this triage to identify what went wrong and dispose of it properly.
1 Which preparation did you make?
🥛LAB Serum
🟣PSB Solution
🌾EM / AEM Ferment
🍚Trichoderma Rice
🥣Bacillus
🧫Aspergillus
🌊Aquatic (BGA / Azolla)
🍋Eco Enzyme (环保酵素)
❓Other / Unsure
2 What does it look / smell / feel like? (tap all that apply)
Smell
Colour
Texture / Surface
3 Diagnosis & Disposal Protocol
Choose disposal scale
📌 Universal Safety Rules
• Never pour failed brews into drains, streams, ponds or rice paddies — kills fish and contaminates groundwater.
• Never apply to food crops within 30 days of harvest, regardless of how it looks.
• Never dump untreated into an active compost pile — bad microbes can take over.
• Never burn liquid brews — releases bioaerosols that can be inhaled.
• Always wear gloves and a mask when handling spoiled brews — even low-risk batches.
• Always wash containers with hot soapy water + 1% bleach rinse before reusing.
💡 UV PROTECTION FOR HOME FERMENTS: If you're fermenting UV-sensitive fungi (Trichoderma, Beauveria, Metarhizium) or sensitive bacteria at home, add Sodium Lignosulfonate to the wet ferment before drying: 10g per 1kg of substrate, OR 100g per 100L of liquid ferment. This extends shelf-life and protects from UV degradation after foliar application. See Tank-Mix Builder for spray timing guidance.

🪣 Tank-Mix Compatibility Checker

Check if microbes, fertilisers, and pesticides can go in the same tank — without killing each other or precipitating. Dosage is not the focus here; for that, use the AI Farm Advisor.

1. Add components
Common water-soluble foliar fertilisers, adjuvants and biostimulants. Default amounts pre-set per litre × tank size — adjust after adding.
Common active ingredients used in Malaysian agriculture. Listed by chemical name (not brand) — many of these KILL beneficial microbes.
↕
📋 Pick the chemistry classification. Pesticide labels in Malaysia print the FRAC, IRAC, or HRAC code by regulation. This lets the compatibility engine reason about your product even if it is not in our catalogue.
If unsure, always check the product label first.
Pick the element your fertiliser delivers (Ca / Mg / N / P / K / micronutrient) and the chemical form. The compatibility engine uses these to flag ion clashes (e.g. Ca + phosphate = precipitate).
Surfactant ionic class matters for microbial mixes. Cationic types kill bacteria via membrane disruption. Most agricultural stickers are non-ionic (safe).
⚠ Compatibility cannot be auto-checked. The engine has no chemistry data for this item, so other warnings (kills microbes, ion clashes) WILL NOT fire for this entry. Do a jar test (mix 1:1 with tank water, wait 15 min — cloudiness or sediment = incompatible) before mixing in the field. Always verify the product label.
2. Your mix — compatibility
3. Field mixing sequence
4. Save or share
